Houle of Commons in this preſent PALLIAMENT, —
Much bonored Sir: YH —
22 — this Dedicarory Epiſtle of mine
| liſhed without your knowledge, p hall beget no ſuch ſiniſter conſtructi-
on in you, but that the fault ſhall be
admitted as a veniall tranſgre ſſion;
and this my preſumption finde cafe
remiſſion at your moſt gentle hands.
Im now ſo well acquainted wich your pleaſing native Diſpoſition, that in things of this nature where you are not in queſtion, I dare a little offend , for its a fixed na- turall Maxime ingraffed in you, to love your friends ſim- cerely, and rarely to take offence vpon flight failings.
Pardon this boldneſſe; verily, 5 mauy, ſo number-
leſſe are my engagements unto you, that I could doe ro left. dav ing to other meanes remaining whereby to ex-
A 3 preſſe
—   Bchold Aſtrologie,
Now Phezrx like, revivd againe in thee / Queſtions reſolv'd, Nativities, Directions, Tranſites, with Revolutions and Profections. Saturne muſt lay his ſullen prankes aſide,
And Mars his madneſſe, leſt he be deſcride; Vienus her luſts; his thefts muſt Mercury;
Sol his ambition ; Joe his jollity:
Luna her fickle and unconſtant motion,
Is now notorious to each vulgar notion.
Ake what you will, Would: you reſolved be > Obſerve your time, learne your Nativirie :
Were Prem, Chambers, Perkins, Melton, Geree, Vica-s, to write againe, all men would jecr yee. You durſt nor ler us know when you were borne, Your ignorance is brought to publick ſcorn :
Our Latin Lich is for Boyes are young;
Our Engliſh Li is for Men more ſtrong.
The Sy Books were burner, they are all gone
I will preſerve my choyce, This is that one :
Be you for or againſt, or will ye, nill ye.
I'm for ti. Art, aud th Author William Lilly,
| .. Ce Joun Boo RER.
Upon the learned Worke of the
AUTHOR.
Ehold Urania with a Lily deckt, Preſents her ſelfe to Englands gracious view. Let Envies ſquare, or oppoſite aſpect Not dare at her a frowning looke to ſhew ; Leſt ir be ſaid, for ſuch ungratefull ſcornes, A Lilly late hath ſprung among che thornes.
WILL, ROE,

Or ro commend the Author, tis the leaſt Of all my thoughts, this Work will doe it beſt; Nor yer to vex the prying Readers ſence with bumbaſt words inſtead of Eloquence, Doe I crowd in theſe rude unpoliſht lines: But rather to informe the giddy times How much they are his Debtors ; what they owe To him, whoſe Labonrs freely doth beſtow On chem his Art, his paines, his piercing ſight, His lampe of life, to give their darkneſſe light. Tis now a crime, and quite grown out of faſhion, T'incourage Art amongſt the Engliſh Nation. Tell chem — it, or Natures myſteries, Tuſh, cry they: —— they idolize. The glorious Stars, they think God doth not uſe them To doe his will : Lord / how doe men abuſe them? Nor will allow the Planets co fulfill (As inftruments) Gods high decree or will. Nay, ſome there are, though letter wiſe, they can Not yet beleeve that all wa- made for Man. Barke black mouth d Envie; carpe at what's well done, This Booke ſhall be my choyce companion.

There be alſo twelve Signes : Aries V, Taurw & , Cemini U, Cancer S, Leo K, Virgo M, Libra , Scorpio mM, Sagittarius 2 Caprice Ws, Aquarius , Piſces % : Through thele twelve Signes the Planets continually move, and are ever in one or other degree of them. It's neceſſary you can perſectly diſtinguiſh the character of every Planet and Signe, before you proceed to any part of this ſtudy ; and alſo t e characters of theſe Aſpects that follow, viz, * a fg. 4

An Introduction to Afirologie.
You muſt know, every Signe containes in [i thirry degrees, and every degree ſixty minutes, &c. the is from V, and ſo in order one Signe after another: ſo the whole Zodiack containes 360. degrees, the ſecond of N is the the two and thirtieth degree of the Zodiack, the tenth of v5 is

and would know to what degree of the Ecliptick he caſteth his (iniſter Sextil Aſpect; rekoning from Y to the tenth de- gree of U, 1 find h to be in the ſeventieth degree of the Zodi- ack, according to his longitude; if adde fixty degrees more to ſeventy, they make one hundred and thirty, which anſwers to the tenth degree of the Signe , to which h caſteth his + Aſpet, or to any Planet in that degree.
When two Planets are equally diſtant one from each other, ſixty degrees, we ſay they are in a Scætill Aſpect, and note it wich this character + .
When two Planets are ninety degrees diſtant one from ano- ther, wee call that Aſpect a Qaartil Aſpect, and write it thus, G.
When Planets are one hundred and twenty degred diſtant, we lay they are in a Trine Aſpect, and we write it thus a.
When two Planets are one hundred and eighty degrees dl - ſtant, we call that AſpeR an Oppoſition, and character the Aſp:& thus Pf.
When two Planets are in one and the ſame degree and mi- nute of any Signe, we ſay they are in Conjundtion, and write it thus & .
So then if you find h̊ in the firſt degree of V, and ꝙ or any other Planet in the firſt degree of U, you ſhall ſay they are in a Seætill Aſpect, for they are diſtant one from another fixty de- grees, and this Aſpe ct is indifferent good,
If Þ or any other Planet be in the firk degree of V, and ano- ther Planet in the ſicſt degree of , you mult ſay they are in a
CG Afpet, becauſe there is ninety degrees of the Zodiack be- twixt them: this Aſpect is of emnity and not

An Introduci ion to Aitrologie. * degree of d, there being now the diſtance of an hundred and twenty degrees, they behold each other with a Trine Aſpect; and this doth denote Unity, Concord and Friendſhip.
If you find h in the firſt degree of V, and any Planet in the firſt degree of , they being now an hundred and eighty de- grees each from other, are ſaid to be in Oppoſition : A bad Aſpect: and you muſt be minctull to know what Signes are oppolite each to other, for without it you cannot erect the Figure.
When h; is in the firſt degree of Y, and any Planet is in the ſame degree, they are then ſaid to to be in Conjanttion : And this Aſpect is good or ill, according to the nature of the queſti- on demanded,
Signes Oppoſite to one an another are

That is V is oppoſite to =, and to Y; & tom, tox: and ſoin order as they ſtand. | would have all men well and readily apprehend what pre.
which is no other thing, then a Book containing the true pla- ces of thePlanets,in degrees and minutes, in every of the twelve Signes both in longitude and latitude, every day of the yeer at noon, and every hour of the day, by correftion and equation.
| have inſerted an Ephemeris of the moneth of Tama 1646 and after it a Table of Houſes for the latitude of 52. degrees, which will terve in a manner, all the Kingdome of England on this ſide Newark, upon Trent, without ſenſible errour; and this I have done of purpoſe to teach by them, the uſe of an Ephe- meris, and the manner and meanes of erecting a Figure of Hea- ven, without / hich nothing can be knowne or made ule of in Aſtrology.

| 28 An Introdudi ion to Aftrolegie.
In the ſecond line you find, The daily motions of the Pla nets and the Dragons head.
In the third line and over the character of h you have M. D. M. ſignifying Meridional/, D. Deſcending ; that is, h hath Meri- dionall latitude, and is Deſcending. -
In the next column you find M. D. and underneath u; that is, Jupiter hath South or leridionall-latiende, an4 is deſ- cending..
In the third column you find M. A. and nnder thoſe letters & 5 that is, F hath Meridionaitlaticude, and is aſcending.
The © hath never any laticude.

Of the daily motion of the Planett, and bow to reduce their motion to any baure of the day, and to te M.ri lian of London.
E have ſeldome occaſion to erect a Scheame of Heaven juſt at noon, to which time the motions of the Planets are exactly calculated, and need not any rectiſication y but uſu- ally all Queſtions are made either ſome hours before, or after noon ztherfore it is needſull you know how to take their diur- nall or daily motion, or how many degrees or min, they move in 24 hours, that thereby you may have a proportion to adde to the place of your Planets according to the hour of the day or night when you ſet your Figure: And although in horary Queſtions, it occaſionetk no error (except in the motion of the Þ) yet | chought fit to inſtruſt the Learner herewith, that ſo he may know how to doe his work handſomely. Example. You mult ſet downe the place of your Planet in Signe, de- gree and minute as you find him at Roon ; and if your Planet ba direct, you muſt ſubſtract him in degree and minute from the place he is in the day ſubſequent: but when a Planet is retro- gods, you mult doe the contrary, viz. ſubſtract the motion of voue Plant the day ſubſequent from the day going before.

) CAT. VI. Of the twelve Heu el of Heaven, and ſome Names or Termes of Af 7 olopie, | He whole Sphegre of Heaven is divided into four equall parts by the Meridian and Horizon, and againe into fone Quadrants
5
48 An Introduci ion to Aftro logie.
Quadrants, and every Quadrant againe into three parts, ac- cording to other Circles draw ne by points of Sections of the aforeſaid Meridian and tiorizon; ſo the whole Feaven is di- vided into twelve equall parts, which the Aſtrologers call Houſes or, Manſions, taking their beginning from the Eaſt.
The firſt Quadrant is deſcribed from the Eaſt to the Mid- heaven, or from the line of the firſt houſe to the line of the tenth houſe, and containes the twelfth, eleventh and tenth houſes, i, called the Oriental Vernall, Ma) online, Sangwine, Infant Juarter.
The ſecond Quadrant is from the Cuſp of the Mid. heaven to the Cuſp of the ſeventh houſe, containg the ninth, eighth and — houſes, and is called the Meridian, Eſlival, Feminine, Icuthfull, Cholerich quarter.
The third Quadrant is from the Cuſp of the ſeventh houſe to the Cuſp ot the fourth houſe, and containes the ſixth, fifth and fourth houſes, is called Occidentall, Autumnall, Maſculine, Melancbollique, Manbood, cold and dry.
The fourth Quadrant is from the Cuſp of the fourth to the Cuſp of the firit houſe, and cantaines the third, ſecond and
firſt houſe, is Nertberne, Feminine Old age, of the nature of Winter, +
Pblepmatique. .
The firſt, tenth, ſeventh and fourth houſes hereof are called gles, the ele venth, ſecond, eight and ſiſt are called Succedanti, the third, twelfth, ninth and ſixth, are tearmed Cadents : the An- gles are moſt powerfull, che Succedants are next in vertue, the Cadents poore, and of little efficacy : the Succedant houſes fol- low the | 8s Cadents come next the Succedants zin force and vertue they ſtand ſo in order:
110 7 4 11 593 28 6 12
The meaning whereof is this, that two Planets equally digni- fed, the one in the Aſcendant, the other in the tenth houſe, you ſhall judge the Inet in the Atcendant ſomewhat of more power to effect what he is Significator ot, then he that is in the tenth : doe ſo in the reſt as they ſtand in order, remembring that Planers in Angles doe more forcibly ſhew their effefts. When we name the Lord of the Aſcendant, or Significator ; of
An Introd«Sion to Aftrologie. 49 of the Qucrent, or thing queſited ; we meane no other thing then that Planet who is Lord of that Signe wh'ch aſcends, or Lord of chat Signe from which houſe the thing demanded is re- quired ; as if from the ſeventh houſe, the Lord of that Signe deſcending on the Cuſp is Significator, and ſo in the reſt : hut of this in the enſuing Judgments.
Cofienificator is when you find another Planet in aſpect or conjunction wich that Planet who is the principall Signifi- cator ; this ſaid Planet ſhall have ſigniſication more or leſſe, and cither aſſiſt or nat in effecting the thing deſired, and io hath ſomething to doe in the Judgment, and ought to be con» Udered: if a Friendly Planet, he notes good ; it an infortune the contrary, viz. either the deſtruction of a thing, or diſtur- bance in it,
Almuten, of any houſe is that Planet who hath moſt dignities in the Signe aſcending or deſcending upon the Cuſp of any houſe, whereon or from whence you require your judgment.
Almuten of a Figure, is that Planet who in Eflentiall and Acci- dentall dignities, is moſt powerfull in the whole Scheame of Heaven.

Cnuae. VII. Of the twelve Hy es, their Nature and ſigniſi cation. As before we have ſeid chere are twelve Signes, and alſo twelve Houſes of Heaven, ſo now we are come to relate the nature of theſe twelve Houſes ; the exact knowledge where- of is ſo requilite, that he who ſha'! learne the nature of the Plancts and Signes wichout ext judgment of the Houſes, is like an improvident man, that furnĩ ſhetk himſclfe with varicty of Houſholdituffe, having no place wherein to beſtow them. There is nothing appertaining to the lite of man in this world, which in one way or other hath not relation to one of the twelve Houſes of Heaven, and as the twelve Signes are ap- propriate to the particular members of mans body ; ſo alſo doe the twelve houſes repreſ:nt not onely the ſeverall parts of man, but his actions, quality of life and living, and the curiotity and judgment of our Fore-fathers in Aſtrology, was ſuch, as they have alotted to every houſ a particular ſignification, and 10 diſtinguiſhed humane accidents throughout the whole twelve houſes, as he that underitands the Qneſtions appertainiag to each of them, ſhal not want ſutticient grounds wheron to judge or give a rationall anſwer upon any contingent accident, and - ſuccelle thereot,

CMA. XI. Of the Sun, and bis generall and particular ſignifications. *
He Sun is placed in the middle of all the Planets, and is Sol. called amongſt the Ancients, both Poets and Hiſtorians, - Fel, Titan, Ilios, Pbebus, Apollo, Pean, Ohyris, Diefpiter : ICs need- leſſe to mention his Colour, being ſo continually viũble to all mortall men: He paſſeth through all the twelve Signes of the Lodiack in one yeer, or in 365 dayes and certaine hours: His meane motion is 59 83 yet his diurnall motion is ſometimes Motion. 57" 16 ſeconds, ſometimes more, never exceeding 61 minutes and ſix ſeconds. He alwayes moves in the Ecliptick, and is ever voyd of lati- tude, ſo that it is very improper in any Aſtrologian to ſpeak of the © his latitude. He hath onely the Sigue of S for his Houſe, and = for his Houſe- Detriment. He is Exalted inthe 19 degree of V, ind receives his Fall in

He js naturally Hot, Dry, but more temperate then & is a Maſculine, Diurnall Planet, Equivalent, if well dignitied to a Fortune,
Very faithfull, keeping their Promiſes with all punctuality, |
a kind of icching deſire to Rule and Sway where he comes: Prudent,and of incomparable Judgment ; of great Majeſty and Statelineſſe, Induſtrious to acquire Honour and a large Patri- mony, yet as willingly departing therewith againe ; the Solar man uſually ſpeaks with gravity, but not many words, and thoſe with great confidence and command of his owne affecti- on; full of Thought, Secret, Truſty, ſpeaks deliberately, and notwithſtanding his great Heart, yet is he Affable, Tractable, and very humane to all people, one loving Sumptuouſneſſe and Magnificence,and whatever is honourable ; no ſordid thoughts can enter his heart, &c.
Then the Solar man is Arogant and Proud, diſdaining all men, cracking of his Pedegree, he is Pur-blind in Sight and Judgment, reſtleſſe, troubleſome, domincerning, a meer va- pour, expenſive, fooliſh, endned with no gravity in words, or ſoberneſſe in Actions, a Spend-thrifr, waſting his Patrimony, and hanging after on other mens charity, yet thinks all men are bound to him, becauſe a Gentleman borne.

The Head of the Dragon is Maſculine, of the nature of * and 2 and of himſelte a Fortune; yet the Ancients doe (ay, that being in & with the good he is good, and Ta & with the evill Planets they account him evill.
The Tayle of the Dragon is Feminine by nature, and cleane contrary to the Head; for he is evill when joyned with good Planets, and good when in conjunction with the malignant Planets. This is the conſtant opinion of all the Ancients, bur upon what reaſon grounded | know not ; I ever found the & equivalent to either of the Fortunes, and when joyned with the evill Planets to leſſen their malevolent ſignification ; when joyned with the your to increaſe the good promiſed by them For the Tayle of the Dragon, I alwayes in my praftile found when he was joyned with the evill Planets; their malice or the evill intended — was doubled and trebled, or extreamly augmented, &c. and when he chanced to be in conjunctionwith any of the Fortunes who were ſigniſicators in the queſtion, though the matter by the principall ſigniũcator was fairely promiſed, and litely to be perfeCted in a ſmall time; yet did there ever fal out many ri bi and diſturbances,much wrangling and great controverſie, that the buſineſſe was many times given over for deſperate ere a perfect concluſion could be had; and unleſſe the principa!l ſignificators were Angular and well for- tified with eſſentiall diqpities, many times unexpectedly the whole matter came to nothing.

Every one of thele Signes containes thirty degrees or parts in longitude : Hense it comes to paſle that the whole —— doth conſiſt of 36 degret s, every degree containes 60 minutes, which we alſo call ſcruples, every minute containes 69 ſeconds, and ſo further if you pleaſe, &c. but in Aſtrology we onely make uſe of degrees, minutes and ſeconds.

Cur, XVII. F Teich ing what uſe may be made of the former Diſcouſe of the
twelve Signes»
1 one demand of the Artiſt, of what condition, quality or
ſtature the perſon queſited, or enquired of is, then obſerve the Signe of that houſe whereby he is ſignified, the Signe wherein the Lord of that houſe is, and wherein the Moone is, mix one with another, and by the greater teſtimonies judge ; for if the Signe be humane, aierial,that aſcends or deſcends, and the Lord of that Signe or the 2 in any Sign of the ſame tripli- city or nature, you may judge the Body to be handſome, and the conditions of the party to be ſociable, or he very courte- ous, &c.
If the Quere be concerning a Diſeaſe, and Y be either on the cuſp of the Aſcendant, or deſcending in the ſixt, you may judge he hath ſomething in his Diſeaſe of the nature of V, but what it is, you muſt know by the concurrence of the other lig- nificatars.
If a Country man or Citizen hath loſt or miſſeth any Cat- tle, or any materiall thing in his houſe, let him obſerve in what Signe the Signiticator of the thing is in; if in Y, and it be a Beaſt ſtrayed, or the like, let him ſee what manner of places that Signe directs unto, and let him repaire thither to ſearch, conſidering the quarter of heaven the Signe ſignifies : if it be an unnoveable piece of Goods, that without man or woman cannot be removed, then let him look into ſuch parts of his houſe, or about his houſe as / {ignifies,
If one aske concerning Travell, whether ſach a Country, City or Kingdome will be healthfull or proſperous unto him, yea or no; ſee in the Figure in what Signe the Lord of the Aſ- cendant is in, if che ſigniſicator be fortunate in V, or if v or 2 be therein, he may ſafely travell or ſojourne in ſuch Cities or Countries as the Signeof Y ſents, which you may eali-
ly diſcerne in the abovenamed Catalogue: Thoſe Countries Au ject to the Signe wherein the Infortunes are polited, unlefle

CMA. XVIII. Of the Eſſential Dignities of the Planets,
1 exact way of judicature in Aſtrology is, firſt, by being perfect in the nature of the Planets — Signes.
Secondly, by knowing the ſtrength, ſortitude or debility of the Planets,Significators,and a well poyling of them and their aſpects and ſeverall mixtures, in your t.
Thirdly, by righcly applying the influence of the poſiture of Heaven erected, and the Planets aſpects to one another at the time of the Queſtion,according to natural (and not enforced) maximes of Art; for by how much you endeavour to ſtraine a judgment beyond nature, by ſo much the more you augment
our Errour, 4
A Planer is then ſaid to be really ſtrong when he hath many Eſſential dignities, which are knowne, by his being either in his Houſe, Exaltation, Triplicity, Terme or Face, at time of ere-
Ring the Figure. As for Example: *
In any — of Heaven, if you find a Planet iu any of Eſſential dig thoſe Signes we call his houſe or houſes, he is then eſſentially uiii by Houſes * ſtrong, and we allow for that five dignities ; as h in Y, * in un
* 5 &c.
In judgment, when a Planet or Significator is in his owne houſe, it repreſents a man in ſuch a condition, as that he is Lord ot hi, o vne houſe, eſtate and fortune; or a man wanting very little of the Gods of this world, or it tels yuu the pan is in a very happy ſtate or condition: this will be true, unleſſe the

If he be in thac Signe wherein he is exalted, you may allow him four dignities eflentiall, whether he be neer the very de- gree of his exaltation, yea or not; as & in or in S.
If the fignificator be in his exaltation, and no wayes impe- dited, but Angular ; it preſents a perſon of haughty condition, arrogant, aſſuming more unto him then his due ; Ge it's obſer ved, the Planets in ſome part of the Zodiack doe more evident- ly declare their effects then in others; and I conceive this to be in thoſe Signes and degrees where fixed Starres of the ſame na- ture with the Planet, are more in number, and neerer the Ecliptick.
If he be in any of thoſe Signes which are alotted him for his
Triplicity, he hath allowed him three dignities : but herein you mult be cautious ; as for example: In a Queſtion, Nativi- ty, or-thelike, if you find the © in V, and the Queſtion, or Nativity, or Scheame erected be by night, and you would ex- amine the © his fortitudes, he ſhall have four dignities for being in his exalcation, which continues through the Signe 3 but he ſhall not be allowed any dignity, as being in his tripli city 3 for by night the © ruleth not the fi riplicity, but V + who had he been in place of the ©, a night, muſt have had allowed him three dignities : and this doe generally in all the Planets, ꝙ excepted, who night and day ruleth the watry T riplicity.
A Planet in his triplicity, ſhewes a man modeſtly indued with the Goods and Fortune of this world, one prettily deſ- cended,and the condition of his life at preſent time of the Que- ſtion, to be good; but not ſo, as if in either of the two former dignities.
If any Planet be in thoſe degrees we aſſigne 2 his Termes, we allow him two dignities; as — night, if v be in one, two, three or four, &c. Y, he is then in his owne Termes,and muſt have two dignities therefore; and ſo 2 in as of the firlt eight degrees of , &c.

A Planet having little or no dignity, bus by being in his De- nate or Face, is Fmot like a man ready to be turned out of doores, having much adoe to maintaine himſelfe in credit and reputation: and in Genealgies it repreſents a Family at the laſt pal p, even as good as quite decayed, hardly able to ſupport it ſelfe.

The Trine aſpect conſiſis of 120 degrees, or by a third part of the Circle, for three times an hundred and twenty degree: make the whole Circle, or 360 degrees: It's called a Triangular aſpect, or Trigonall, and if you find ſometimes the — wocrator, 11's as much as a Planet ruling or having dominion in tuch a Triplicity or Trygon 3 for three Signes make oneTry- gon or Triplicity.
An Oppoſition or Diametrall Radiation is, when the Planets EY diſtant 180 degrees, or halfe the Circle from each other,
A Cenjundior, Coition, Syned or Congreſſe (for ſome uſe all theſe words) is, when two Planets are in one and the ſame degree and minute of a Signe : Other new Aſpects I have formerly mentioned in the beginning of this Diſcourſe. You muſt un- derſtand amongſt theſe Aſpects, the Quadrate Aſpect is a ligne of imperfe& emnity ; and that the Oppoſition is an aſpect or ar- gument of perfect hatred ; which is to be underſtood thus: A Queſtion is propounded, Whether two perſons at variance may be
reconciled ? Admit 1 find the two ſigrificatort repreſenting the two Adveriarics, in Q aſpect; I may then judge, — the aſpc is of imperfect hatred, that the matter is not yet ſo farre gone, but there may be hopes of reconciliation betwixt them, the other fipnificators or Planets a little helping. But if I find the maine ſniſica or in oppolition, it's then in nature impoſſi- ble to expect a peace betwixt them till the ſuĩt is ended, if it be a ſuĩt of Law ; untill they have fought, if it be a Challenge.
The Sextill and Trine aſpects are arguments of Love, Unity and Friendſhip ; but the A is more forcible, (viz.) if the two ſigniffcators are in * or A, no doubt but peace may beeafily concluded,
Conjuncl ions are good or bad, as the Planets in & are friends or enemies to one another.

Reception is when two Planets that are ſignificators in any Queſtion or matter, are in each others dignityz as ©inY, and ꝙ in © here is reception of theſe two Planets by Hou- ſes 3 and certainly this is the ſtrongeſt and beſt of all recepti- ons. It may be by triplicity terme or face, or any eſſentiall dig nity; as 2 in /, and ꝙ in vx ; here is reception by ti iplici- ty, if the Queſtion or Nativity be by day: (O in the 24. of and ꝙ in i, the 16, of 11 ;here is reception by terme, & being in the terms of , and ſhe in his termes.
The uſe of this is much; for many times when as the effect · ing of a matter is denyed by the Aſpets, or when the fignifi- cators have no Aſpect to each other, or when it ſeemes very doubtfull what its promiſed by Q or ꝙ of the ſigniticators, yet if mutuall Reception happen berwixt the principall ſigni- ficators, the thing is brought to paſſe, and that without any great trouble, and ſudden — the content of both parties.
A Planet is then ſaid to be Peregrine, when he is in the de- grees of any Signe wherein he hath no eſſentiall dignity : As
h in the t:nth degree of Y , that Signe being not his Houſe, Exaltation, or of his Triplicicy, or he having in that degree either Terme or Faces, he is then ſaid to be Peregrine ; had he been in 27, 28, &c. of Y, he could not be termed Peregrine, becauſe then he is in his owne Terme.

The uſe of the Table. as
Many times it happens, that it is of great concern nent to the Qierent to know, whether a Woman be wich childe of a Male or Female; or whether the Theefe be Man or Woman, &c. When it ſhall fo chance that neither the Angles, or the ſex of the Planet, or the Signes doe diſcover it, but that the teſti- monies are equall ; then if you conſider the degrees of the Signe wherein the 9 is, and wherein the Planet ſigniſicator of the thing or party queſited is, and the degree or the Cufpe of the Houle ſignifying the perſon queſited after; and ſee by the ſecond Column whether they be in Maſculine or Feminine Degrees,you may poyſe your judgement,by concluding a Maſ- culine party, if they be poſited in Maſculine degrees; or Fe- minine, if they be in Feminine degrees. You ſee the firſt eight degrees of 7 are Maſculine,the ninth degree is Feminine, from nine to fifteene is Maſe line, from fifteen to two and twenty is Feminine, from two and twenty to thirty is Maſculine 3 and ſo as they ſtand directed in all the Signes.
The thicd Columne tels you there are in every Signe certain
Degrees, ſome called Light, Darke, Smoakie, Void, &c.the uſe hereof is thus: |
Let a Signe aſcend in a Nativity or Queſtion, if the Aſten- d int be in thoſe Degrees you ſee are called Light, the Chile or querent ſhall be more faire; if the degree aſcending be of thoſe wo Darke, his Complexion ſhall be nothing fo faire,
but more obſcure and darke ; and if he be borne deformed, the
deformity Mall bote and greater ; but if he be deformed
when the 1 of a Yigne aſcend, the imperſection ſhall be more toler
Degree aſcending be in thoſe degrees we call Voyd, be the ve or Qnerent faire or foule, his un- derſtanding will be ; and his judgement leſſe then the world ſuppoſeth, and the more thou conferreſt with him, che greater Eben ſhale thou finde in him. Called by ſome Degrees lame and deficient are thoſe mentioned in the fifth Azimene de- Columne ; the meaning whereof is thus, If in any queſtion grees. you finde him that demands the queſtion, or in a Nativity, if you finde the Native deſective in any men ber, or infected with an inſeperable diſeaſe, halting, blindneſſe, deafneſſe, &c. you may then ſuppoſe the native hath either one of theſe Azimene degr. aſcending at his birth, or the Lord of the Aſcendant, or the 3 in one of them: in a Queſtion or Nativity, if you ſee the Querent lame naturally, crooked, or vitiated in ſome mem- ber, and on the ſodain you can in the figure give no preſent ſa- tisfation to your ſelfe, doe you then conſider the ree al- cending, or Degree wherein the » is in, or the of the Aſcendant, or principall Lord of the Natlvity ꝙ Queſtion, and there is no doubt but you ſhall finde one or gore of them in Azimene degrees. | Degrees in- Theie Degrees ate related in the fifth Column, and tend to creaſing for-—this underſtanding. that if the Cuſpe of the ſggon@ Houle, or it tune. the Lord of the ſecond houſe, or — of fortune be in, any of thoſe degrees, its an argument of much wealth, and that 4 Native or Querent will be rich.

The Uſe and Reaſen of the former Table.
T was well ncere foure yeeres after | had ſtudied Aſtrology,
before I could finde any reaſon, why the Planets in every ot
the Signes thould (ignifie the members as mentioned in the Ta-

the meaning of it,viz.Erit im-pedimentum circa illam partem corporis
quam ſignificat ſianum, guodfucrit nativitatis tempore impeditum.There
wil be lome impediment in or neer that part of the body, which
is fignified by theSigne that ſhall be afflicted at ume ot the Birth The ule of all comes to thus much :
That if you would know where any Diſeaſe is, I meane in what member of the body, ſee in hat dne the fienicater of the ſick Party is, and what part WF none body that Planet ſig- nifies in that Signe, which you may doe by the former Table, in that member or part of body (hall you ſay the lick party is

An IntroduStion to Aftrologie. 127 Queſtion in I; have recourſe to your Table, and you ſee h̊ in L fi aDiſeaſe in the Belly or heart, xc. Do ſo in the reſt.
ow the reaſon of this lignitication of every Planet in ſuch or ſuch a Signe is this:
Every Planet in his owne Houſe or Signe, governeth the Head; in the ſecond Signe from his Houle, the Neck; in the the third Signe from his Houſe, the Armes and Shoulders ; and ſo ſucceſſively through the twelve Signes : as h in y ruleth the Head, in = the Neck, in Armes and Shoulders: ſo u in i ruleth the Head, in vw the Neck, in =: the Armes and Shoulders.
The 2 obſerves the ſame order as the reſt ; yet the Arabiazy, from whom this learning is, doe allow her in Y the Head as well as the Knees : The Head, becauſe Aries ſignifies ſo much: The Knees, becauſe Aries is the ninth Sign from Cancer.
You may obſerve this in the marks ot mans Body, and ma- ny other } ts, and make ſingular uſe of it ; ever remem- bring this, the more the Signe is vitiated, the greater mole or ſcarre 3 or the neerer to an Azimene, Pitted or deficient degree of the Signe, the ſtronger is the deformiry, ſickneſſe, &c.
CoxnSIDERATIONS before Judgment.
ALL the Ancients that have wrote of Queſtions, doe give
warning to the Aftrologer, that before he deliver judgment he well conſider whether the Figure is radicall and capable of J the Queſtion then ſhall be taken for radicall, or fit to be judged, when as the Lord of the hour at the time of propoling the Queſtion, and — Figure, and the Lord beche Aſcendant or firſt Houſe, are of one Triplicity, or be one, or of the ſame nature.
A. for example 3 let the Lord of the houre be ꝙ, let the Signe of m S orX aſcend, this Queſtion is then radicall, becauſe & is Lord of the hour, and of the Watry Triplicity, or of thoſe
S Nor &. e, let the Lord of the hour be &, and Y aſcend, the Qu (hall be radicall, becauſe ꝙ is both Lord of the hour
and Signe a =
122 Introduction to Aſtrolog
Let the Lord of the hour be & , and let the Signe & aſcend, here, although the © is one of the Lords of the hery Friplici- ty, and ſole Lord of the Signe ©, | or ſhall the Queſtion be judged ; becauſe the ©, who is Lord of the Alcendant, and & who is Lord of the hour, are both of one nature, viz. Conſider what houſe the Planet — or tranſlating the nature and light of the two Planets is Lord of,and deſcribe him or her, and ſay to the party, that ſuch a party ſhall doe good in the buſineſſe of, &c. viz. if Lord of the ſecond, a good Purſe effects the matter; if Lord of the third, a Kinſman or Neighbour; and fo of all the reſt of the Houſes: of which more ſhall be ſaid in the following Judgments.

What Marke, Mole or Scarre the Querent bath in any Member of bis Body. .
have many times admired at the verity hereof, and it hath boen one maine argument of my engaging ſo farre in all the pon of Aſtrology, for very rarely you {ball find theſe rules
ale.
When you have upon any dem ind ere ted the querents Fi- gure, conſider the Sign — what member of mans body it repreſents, and cell che quzrent he Rath a Mole, Scar or marke on th it part of his body repreſented by that Signe ; as if the Signe aſcending be g , ics on the Neck: it in U, on the Arms,

all manner of Queſtions: 149 Lord of the Aſcendant is in, and in that member repreſented by that Signe, he or ſhe hath another.
Then obſerve the Signe deſcending on the Cuſp of the ſixt houſe, and what part of mans body it perſonates, for in that member ſhall you find another; ſo ſkall you diſcover another in that member which is lignified by the Signe wherein the Lord of the ſixt is,
Laſt of all, con ider what Signe the Avon is in, and what member of mans body ir denotes, therein ſhall you alſo find a Mark, Mole or Scarre : if h fignifiethe Mark, it's a darkiſf, obſcure, black one; it ,, then it's uſually ſome Scarre or Cut it he be in a fiery Signe, or elſe in any other Signs, a red mole ; and you niſt alwayes know, that if either the Signe, or the Planet ſignifying the Mole, Mark or Scarre, be much afflicted, the Mark or Scarre is the greater and more eminent.
If the Signe be Maſculine, and the Planet Maſculine, the Mole or Scar is on the right ſide of the body.
The contrary judge, if the Signe be Feminine, and the Lord thereof in a Feminine Signe, |
If the Significator of the Scarre or Mole be above the earth, (that is, from the Cuſp of the Aſcendant to the Cuſp of the ſe- venth, as either in the twelfth, eleventh, tenth, ninth, eighth or ſeventh) the Mark is on the fore part of the body, or viſi- ble to the eye, or on the out (ide of the member; but if the Sig- nificator be under the earth, -viz. in the firſt, ſecond, third, fourth, fift, ſixt, the Mole or Scarre is on the back part of the body, not vilible, but on the inſide of the member.
It few degrees of a Signe doe aſcend, or if the Lord of the Signe be in fe y degrees, the Mole, Mark or Scarre is in the up- per hart »f the member; it the middle of the Signe aſcend, or the Lord thereof in the middle, or neer the middle of the Signe, the Mole or Murk is ia the member, viz. in the middle : It the latter degrees aſcend, or the M, or Lord of the firlt or ſixt houſe be neer the laſt degrees of the Sign, the Mole, Mark or Sc ir is nette the lo er part of the member.

In November and December, when Signes of ſhort afcenſtions are in the Aſcendant, you muſt be wary, for in regard many times the © is not then vihble, and Clocks may faile, it's poſſible you may be deceived, and miſſe of a right Aſcendant, for * and / doe each of them aſcend in the ſpace of three quar- ters of an hour, and fome few minutes; = and in one hour and ſome odds minutes; but if you have the time of the day exact, you need not ever miſtcuſt the ve: icy of your 2 : which will infinitely ſatisſie any that are Students ierein, and cauſe them to take great pleaſure in the Art, and make them ſenſible, that there is as much lincerity in all the whole Art of Afrologic, when it is rightly underſtod and pra- Qized, which at this day I muſt conteſſe it is by very few.
As theſe rules will hold certaine upon the body of every que- rent, and in every queſtion, ſo will they upon the body of the que ſited, (mutatis , mutandis ;) as if one enquires ſomewhat concerning his Wife, then the Signe of the ſeventh houſe, and
*the Signe wherein the Lord of the ſeventh is, ſhall ſhew the Womans Marks; ſo ſhall the Signe upon the Cuſp of the twelfth, for that is the ſixth from the ſeventh, and the Signe wherein the Lord of the twelfth is in, ſhew two more Moles or Marks of the Woman.
Uſually an Infortune in the Aſcendant blemiſhes the Face with ſome Mole or Scarre according to his nature, for the firſt houſe ſignifies the Face, the ſecond the Neck, the third the Armes and Shoulders, the fourth the Breſt and Paps, the fifth the Heart, &c. and ſo every houſe and Signe in order, accor- ding to ſucceſſion 3 for what Signe ſoever is in the Aſcendant, yet in every Queſtion the firſt houſe repreſents the Face: Many times if the) be in ꝙ or ꝙ ofthe ©, the querent hath ſome blemiſh or the like near one of his Eyes; and this is ever true, if the S or & be in Angles, and either of them have any ill aſpe& to Mars.

Whether one abſent be dead or alive.
Tf a Queſtion be demanded of one abſent in a generall way, and the querent hath no relation to the party; then the firſt Houſe, the Lord of that Houſe and the ) ſhall lignifie the ab- ſent party 3 the Lord of the eight Houſe or Planet poſited ei- ther in the Houſe or within five degrees of the Cuſpe of the 87 Houſe (hall ſhew his death or its quality.
In judging this Queſtion, ſee firſt whether the Lord of the Aſcendant, the) and Lord of the eight Houſe or Planet in the eight houſe be corporally joyned together; or that the) Lord of the Aſcendant and Lord of the eight are in oppoſition either in the eight and ſecond, or twelfth and ſixt, for theſe are arguments the party is deceaſed, or ſick, and very neer death.
See allo if there be any tranſlation of the light of the Lord ofthe Aſcendant unto the Loid of the eight, eſpecially in de- grees deepe, lame or deficient ; or on the contrary, that there be any tranſlation or carrying the vertue or influence of the Lord of the eight unto the Lord of the Aſcendanr ; or if, the Lord of the eight be poſited in the Aſcendant, or if the Lord of the Aſcendant and the » be placed in the fourth Houſe, theſe are teſtimonĩes the party abſent is dead.
If the Lord of the Aſcendant be tep-rated from a bad Aſpect of the Lord of the ſixt, you may ſay the abſent hath been late- ly ſicke ; if from the Lord of the eight, he hath been in dinger of death, but is not dead; it from the Lord of the twelfth, he hath been lately much troubled in minde, in feare of impriſun- ment, arrelts,&c, if from tlie Lord of the ſecund, he hath been hard put to it for money, or in diitreſſe tor want; if from the Load ot the ſeventh, in ſome quarrell or contention; it from the Lord of the ninth or third, he hath been croſſed in his journey (if he was at Sea by contrary windes,or Pyrats) if at Land by Theeves, bad Wayes, &c. and ſo of the reſt. In judg- ing this que tion, l have ever found, that it the Lord of the Aſcendant be in the ninth, tenth, or eleventh (though many reports went the abſent was dead)yer I found him to live. In December 1 644;
A HA TC © 3 | a Merchant in Londen . e: having ſent out — . 4 . Ship to the Coats
of Spaine for Trade, had ſeverall times newes that his Ship was loſt or caſt a- way, there havin been a little before very Tempeſtuous weather, in ſo much that many Shippes were ſunk and ſhip- wrackt ; he would
. have given 601i, in the hundred for the aſſurance of her; ſo generall was the report of her loſſe, that none of the Enſurance company would meddle, no not upon any tearmes. A Friend of the Merchant! propounds the Queſtion unto me, Wat I thought of the Sbip, if ſunk or living? whereupon | erected the Figure preceding, and having well conſidered what was requiſite in this manner of Judgment, I gave my Opinion, That the Ship was not loft, but did live, and though of late in ſome danger, yet was now recovered. My Judgment was grounded upon the Conkderations in Art fol- lowing.

Of the time of receiving any Q.u #3 T1 0.
It hath been diſputed Jargely amangſt. the Arabiant, who - were excellent in the reſolution -of Horary Queſtions, what time the Arologer ſhould take for the ground of his Queſtion, whether that time when the Quzrent fuſt comes into ones Houſe or Cloſet, or firſt flutes the Artiſt, whether that is to be admitted forthe moſt proper time of erecting a Figure, and giving Judgment thereupon. « Though ſome have conſented to this opinion, yet I could never be ſatisfied herein either with reaſon or experience: for let us admit one comes to demands a Reſolution of me, and we . converſe together a good while ; but in the end, ſome occaſion intervenes, and we depart: I hope no ſound Judgment wil allow of this time to be the Radix of a Queſtion (whenas none is really demanded) viz. at what time he teſt ſaw me, or entred my houſe and ipake with me,
Without doubt the true houre of receiving any Queſtion is then, when the Querent propounds his deſire unto the Arolo- ger, even that very moment of time, in my opinion, is to be ac-
: for let js cm ay ſent or delivered unto me, wherein 1 am defired to reſolve ſome doubts ; perhaps 1 receive the Letter into my hands at three of clock in any day of the week, but in regard of ſome occaſions, doe not — it untill
four or five hours after; that very hour and minute of hour , when l break it open, and perceive the intention of the Querent, is the time to which I ought to erect my. Fi and from thence to draw mine Aſtrological Judgment: This way and man- ner have | practiſed, and found ſucoeſſe aniwerable : And whereas Bonatus and ſome others doe give warning that the Alrologian judge not his owne Queſtion, and ſay he cannot tell how to accept of a queſtion from himſelfe ; chis I conceive was his reaton, Becauſe he thought the Artiſt would be partiall to himſelfe in his judgment: Verily I am of a contrary opinion
aud have found by many experiments, that at what m—_— 1

184 The Reſolution of lent repute, have many good Friends and Acquaintance, b whoſe meanes he ſhoul — his Eſtate: And the — why 1 judged augmentation of his Wealth by means of Friends was, becauſe 9 is ſeated on the cuſp of the eleventh houſe, which ſignifiech Friend(hip, &c. for in all —— you muſt warily conſider the nature of the houſe wherein the applicati- on of your Significators are, &c. as alſo,when you judge in this nature of rhings contingent, you muſt meaſure ont the time when they ſhall happen according to reaſon, and mix Art and | Reaſon together, and not too much rely upon the generall rules of Art, for Abs te & a Scientia.
Of that Planet or Planets impediting the effe ing or performance of what is demanded in every Queſtion.
It is conſiderable in all Demands, that you be careſull to know what that Planet is, who impedites the matter, or hin- ders it, that it ſhall take no effect, and we may juſtly call him Strong, Hurtſull, Deſtroyer, Abſciſſor, becauſe he onely and perverts the nature of the Queſtion, when otherwayes it would come to a good concluſion : We receive judgment here- in from that Planet with whom the Lord of the Aſcendant is joyned, or the Significctor of the thing queſited after, whether it be the her ſelſe, or that ſhe is-partaker with the Lord of the - or no, or is Sign cri of the thing De- mande

Whether the Birth ſbal be by day or by night ?
In this manner of Judgment, behold the aſcendant and his
Lord, the Moon, Planet in the aſcendant, Lord of the fifth, Sign of the fifth ; if the major — of the Significaters be in Diurnall Signes, the Birth will be by day; iſ the contrary happen, then in the night. If the Significators diſagree amonglit themſelves, take him that is Efſentially moſt ſtrong, and judge by him ; or elſe conſider the number of degrees that the Planet you judge by is diſtant from the cuſp of the fifth houſe, ſo many degrees as is their diſtance each from other, doe you project from the degree aſcending, and ſee where your number determines ; and if itend in a Diurnall Signe, ſhee will be brought to bed by day; if contrary then in the _ : by this meanes alſo yon may judge of the quality of the Sex, by confidering the Lord of the fitth, the Moone the Lord of the Houre, and the Part of Children before mentioned, and his Diſpoſitor; if the major part hereof be in Maſculine Signes, its a Male the Woman goes with, and the birth will be by day; but if the teſtimonies be equall, the birth will be by twilight. þ Some lay, it the Queſtion be, Whether a Woman be with Chile ar not 3 contider the Lord of the Aſcendant, Lord of the fifth, and Diſpolitor of the Mone, and the Moore her ſelfe ; if any ap- plication be berwixt theſe Planets, and the M.on be in a com- mon Signe, and the Afcendant one, and the Significaters in An- gles, or if in the aſcendant or ſecond there be a fortunate Pla- net, (he is with child, otherwiſe not.

Cu Af. XLI. Of Embaſſadours or Meſſengert.

CAP. XLVII. Of the CRYSIS in Diſcaſes. 9 is no other thing then a duell or contention he nature and the infirmity ; if nature 2 © 9
ell manner of Queſtions. 291 . overcome the of the Diſeaſe, ici a good Cryſis ; if the ſickneſſe prevaile, ic's a pernicious and ill Cryſis, Or
Curt is no more then this, viz. A ſudden alteration of man's body when he is fick, tending either to health or further ſickneſſe ; for when this CH: is, there's a (harp: fight, as,it were, betwixt nature and the Diſeaſe, whether of them ſhall overcome,
Dayes Critical,,Decretory and Chryſmal are all one, and intend no more then a certaine and more ſure judgment of the infir- mity affiifting, either more powerfully, or in a leſſe meaſure at thoſe times when the true Chi is.
The true Cryſis is belt of all taken from that moment of time when firſt the ſickneſſe invaded the Infirme; which if it cannot be had, then it may be taken (but not ſo certainly) from the very hour when firſt the Water is brought to the Do- or to adviſe for recovery: but if no Urine come, then when the Doctor firſt ſpeaks with the ſick party, and is demanded by the I med what he thinks of his ſickneſſe, and what courſe he would adviſe for cure thereot,
Every ſudden and vehement motion of the diſcaſe may be called a Cryſis as Galen faith z or it is, not a locall motion alto- gether, but an alteration of the Diſeaſe.
O. Cry/is impores judgment in the diſeaſe affliting, and which way it will terminate, viz. tor good or evill.
Hypecrates will have Cryſis to be an acute or (wift reportation in diſeaſes, either to recovery or death: But, ſay ſome, in regard there are more diſeaſes to terminate in bealib then in death (except pe- ſtilentiall diſeaſes) where the matter and cauſe is ſo malignant and fo, anos, that nature many times doth not attend a fight or combate with tue Diſeaſe, whereby it cannot properly be called a Cryſis; that de finiti- on of Hypocrates will net we hold, wnleſſe it be in ſuch diſcaſes as de: termine in a recavery of the ſick party: So ſome lay.
Avicenn-, in Canticis, agrees with Galen, and faith, Cryſis (eſt velox motus morbi ad (alutem vel ad mortem.)
There are ſome that have contended, That altbough in diſeaſes there is a Cry ſis, yet is it not cauſed by influence ef the Celefliall bedies, bu! from inferiour cauſes, 2

The Significations of the ſeventh Housx.
It ſigniftes Marriage, open Enemies, Law-ſuits, Controverſies, Contrafts, Narres, Bargaines,
Fugitives, Theſts, Oc.
Becauſe the Demands which doe naturally appertain to the ſeventh houſe, require more conſideration, and are more difficult to judge then of any other houſe, I have been euforced to be more large in delivering the opinions of the Ancients, as of {ome moderne Practi- ſers 3 and have alſo publiſhed forty three ſignificant Aphoriſmes ; which, if well underſtood, will give great light, not only for better underſtanding what concernes this houſe, but the whole body of 4ſrels2y.

not ſtolen. Of the age of the Thief,
The age is taken from the Planet that is Significator of the Thiefe, it he be Orientall,he is young ; in the midſt of his Ori- entality, then of middle age; if he be in the end of his Orien- tallicy, he is old, faith Haly.
To judge by the diſtance of the Planets from the © , for by the © the Planets are Orientall and Occidentall, by which the lignification of age is taken, after Haly, and other Writers.

Whether the Thief be of the houſe or not.
If both the Lights behold the aſcendant, or be in their owne houſes, the Thief is one of the Family, the Lord of the ſeventh in the aſcendan”, idem; the Lord of the ſixt in the ſecond, it is a Servant; if either of the Laminaries behold the aſcendant, it is no ſtranger; © oppoſite to the aſcendant, it is an overthwart Neighbour ; the Lord of the ſeventh beholding the aſcendant with a friendly aſpect, idem.

I am perſwaded the Barge-man looked upon the ſack twenty times before, and knew it not, for the woman had waſhed it cleane: I as heavily complained to the woman for ſeven Portygall Onyons which I loſt; ſhe not knowing what they were, made pottage with them, as ſhe ſaid. Whether one abſent will returne or not, and when ?
Conſider by what houſe the abſent party is ſignified; and what Planet is his ſignificator; then ſee if his ſignificator be in the firſt houſe (let his Journey be whither it will,) yet if it be a long Journey, and beyond Seas, then ſee it he be in the ninth, or it in the twelfr, if a very long Journey was undertaken ; or if he be in the fift, if a moderate Journey was intended, or in the third, if a ſhort Journey : If he be in any of theſe houſes, or do commit his diſpoſition to any Planer in any of theſe houſes, it lignifies the abſent will not dye in that Voyage, but returne : it he be in the ſeventh, he will returne, but not in haſt ; nay, he will tarry long; and he is at time of the Queſtion in that country unto which he firſt went, nor hath he hitherto had any thoughts of returning; howſoever, now he hath : If he be in the fourth, he will ſtay and abide longer then it he were in the ſeventh : it his Sipnificator be in the third or ninth, and in any alpe& with any Planet in the aſcendant, the abſent is pre- paring to come home, and is fully reſolved thereof; or if he be in the ſecond, in aſpect with a Planet in the ib, he is endea- vouring to provide moneys tor his Voyage homewards, nor will it be long ere he be at home; but if he be in a Cadent houſe, and not behold his owne aſcendant, he neither cares for his returne, or hath any thoughts thereof, nor can he come if ſo be he would: it he be cadent and alſo afttifted, and behold not the aſcendant, but is otherwayes impedited, there's no hopes of his returne, nor will he ever come; but if either his Significator be Retrograde, or the) joyned to a Retrograde Planet, and b: hold the aſcendant, it imports his ſudden return when not expected: if you find his Significator impedited, ſee what houſe he is Lord of that doth infortunate himzif it be the Lord of the fourth, the man is detained and cannot have liber- ty; if it be the Lord of the ſixt, he is ill; if the Lord of the eight, he feares he ſhall dye by the way, or before he gets into his owne Country; if the Lord of the twelſt, he is as a priſoner and cannot procure liberty : ſuch configurations as theſe ſeem to impede his returne.
Having conſidered the Sigpificator of the abſent, now have re- courle

Conſider it the Lord of the aſcendant be joyned to an Infor- tune, who receives him not either by Houſe or Exalcation, or by two of bis leſſer Dignities, and the ꝙ alſo at that time un- fortunate, it fignihes the Querent's death.
If in like manner you find the Lord of the firſt joyned to the Lord ofthe eighth, unleſſe the Lord of the eighth receive him, and ſo notwithſtanding, as that the Lord of the firſt receive not the Lord of che eighth, though he receive che Lord of che firſt; becauſc if the Lord ot the eighth receive the Lord of the hiſt, and the Lord of the firſt the Lord of the eighth, whether For- tune or Infortune, you may juſtly feare the death; but if the Lord of the eighth — the Lord of the aſcendant, ſo there be not mutuall Reception, it hinders not.

Whether the Man or Wife ſhall dye firſt.
This doth more neerly depend upon the Nativity of either party, then upon an horary Queſtion, and therefore I would adviſe in the reſolution of this Queition, that firſt the Artiſt doe demand of the que rent, his or her age, or if they have it, the time of their Birth, and that he ere& che Fi thereof, and ſce what bility there was in the Radix, of the length or ſhortneiſe ot the querent's lite if time give you leave, fee it the © or p in the Radix, or the aſcendant of the Nativity, doe neer the time of the Queſtion, come to any malignant di- rection, or whether the querent be not in or neer a ClimaQeri- call yeer or yeers, which are the ſeventh, fourteenth, one and twentieth, 28. five and thirtieth,two & fortieth, &c. or whether you find not maleficall tranſits of the infortunate Planets ei- ther by their neer & to the degree of the © » or aſcendant in the Radix, or whether they caſt not their Q or & aſpects to the degrees of the © or aſcendant of the Radix, now at this inſtant time of the Queſtion ; this I would have well conſider- ed: and then ereſt your Figure according to the time of the day given, and behold who azks the queſtion, and let the Lord of the aſcendant be for him or her, the Lord of the ſeventh for the quelited. party; ſee which of them is weak- eft, or moſt aſſſicted in the Figure, and whether the aſcendant or ſeventh houſe hath any malevolent Planet polited therein, or whether there ariſe with the aſcendant, or deſcend with the cuſp of the feventh, any mileficall fixed Starres; for in this manner of judgment they ſhew much: Behold whether the Lord of the ſeventh, or of the aſcendant goe to combuſtion firſt, or to the afflicti un of any malignant Planet, or to the Lord of the eighth ; for it is an altered rule, that if the Lord of the

What wind you ſhall bave.
Behold the Lord of the aſcendant, if he be with a good Pla- net or Planets, and they ſtrong, and in a friendly aſpect, it fig- nitieth good winds z but if with evill Planets, or they in cadent houſes, the contrary 3 if the Significators be in S our of fixed Signes, and neer violent fixed ſtarres, the Traveller may expect impetuous ſtormes, ſudden blaſts, contrary winds —= dri- ving the Paſlenger this way, and ſometimes that way; as alſo, that he ſhall await many dayes, weeks or moneths for a com- fortable wind before he Wal gaine it: Gentle guſts of wind are ſignified when the Significators are in A aſpect out of Signes ayery, viz, IA or =,
Of bim that taketh a Fonrney, and the ſucceſſe thereof.
Behold what Planets are in the angles, if you find in the afſ- cendant a good Fortune, judge then that he ſhall have good ſuc- ceſſe, before that he removeth from the place he is in, or in the beginning of his Journey ; if that good Fortune be in the Mid- heaven, he ſhall have much happineſſe in his Jeurney, and af- ter his entring the Ship, or upon the way as he on his Journey: but if the Fortune be in the ſeventh, he ſhall have con- tent in the place whereunto he : if that Planer which is the Fortune be in the fourth, it ſhall be at his returne, or when he ſhall come to his one place or home back againe,
If that Fortune be , the benefit he expects, or the Means to advance him, ſhall come from or by meanes of a religious per- ſon, or Judge, or Gentleman, in and kind accordi to the power, quality and nature of the Lord of the houſe that Fortune, or by a perſon of that deſcription ; as if it be the houſe of the O, be by the King, a Magiſtrate, or ſome
noble

If b b: Sipnificater, it ſhall conſiſt in things and Commodities of his Nature, or elſe in things of antiquity, or Ground, Corne or Tillage ; or by meanes of an ancient man: deſcribe h exatly, and let the man apply to ſuch a one in his Affaires. If ©, it ſhall be as touching Women, Joy and Sport, or by meanes of Women, Friends, or in Linnen, Silks, Jewels, or pleaſant things. To what part of Heaven the Traveler bad beſt direft bis Tourney.
Although we have in ſome meaſure handled this judgement in the Chapter of the ficſ houſe, yer I (hall againe briefly men- tion ſome tucther judgements herein, pertinently belonging to this Houle. Hence it is, that when 9 are made for the happineſſe of a party in is going unto, that we alwayes put a good Planet in the eighth houſe. What ſhould become of him ?
His Significator, viz. , being peregrine, and in the third, l laid, it ſhould come to paſſe, he thould be at our diſpoſing, and that we ſhould at laſt have him-in our owne cuſtody, and doe what we liſt with him: this I judged, becauſe the gentle Planet „ Eſſex Significator, did diſpoſe of Rupert: an errour in I confeſle it was, yet not much to be blamed,for ( in totidem ver- bis) it was very neer truth, for in 1646. he was beſieged in Ox- ford, and after ſurrender thereof, having unadviſedly repaired to Oatlands, contrary to Agreement and Covenant, he wat then at the mercy of the Parliament, and in their mercy : but they of that houſe looking on him rather as an improvident young man, then any way worthy of their diſpleaſure ortaking notice of, let him depart with his owne proper fate, heavy enough for him to beare 3 and ſo he eſcaped, So that the generall fate of this Kingdome, overcame my private opinion upon Prince Rupert. However, I am glad he eſcaped (v, being queſtionleſſe a man of able parts, but unfortunate, not in himſelfe, but in the fate of his amily.

Beſides, | cbſerved that & made haſte toa U of 5 in S, as if our Souldiery would quite deſtroy and bring to no- thing her Army ; and that ſhe would be croſſed at or neer the time of that aſpect, which was the 11. of April, when about Notting bam ſhe loſt ſome Forces, and more had, but that we had ever either lome knaves or fools in our Armies.

If they be Cattle that are bewitched, take ſome ef the Haire ef every one of them, and mix the Haire in faire water, or wet it well, and tven 9 it ander the Tile, the Trevet ſtanding over the Tile : make a luiiy fire, turne zur Tile oft upon the Haire, and ſtir up the Hare ever and anon ; after you bave dene this by the pace of a quarter of an bour, let the fire alone, and when the afbes are cold, bury them in the ground towards that quarier of beaven where the ſuſpetted Witch lives,

43 2 The Reſolution of
CAF. XCVIL
To find out what Planet raleth every beur of the Day or Night by the preceding T ables
O U muſt underſtand that as there are ſeven dayes of the
ter, Mars, Sol, Vexus, Mercury, Lana: We appropriate to each day of the Week a ſeverall Planet; as to Sunday, & to Monday, D; to Tueſday, & 3 to IVedneſday, 7 ; to Thurſday, V 3 to Friday, g 3 to Saturday, h: and the ficſt hour of every day we aſſigne to that Planet aſſigned for the day, beginning at Sun-riſe ever, the ſecond houre we give to the next Planet, the third hour to the third Planet from him; as if upon any Swnday I would know what Planet governeth the firſt, ſecond, third, fourth, fift, ſixt hour of that day, I ſay © governeth the firſt, 9 the ſecond, y the third, ; the fourth, h the fift, the fixt, &c. and ſo in order ſucceſſively during that day and night ſubſe- quent: and if you account in order, you ſhall find by this con- tinuall account, that ) fals to rule the firſt hour upon Monde), h the ſecond, Y the third, & the fourth, &c.

Either in giving Phyſick, or performing many naturall conclufions, without exact knowledge of the Aſtrologicall planetary hour, no worthy work can be done, with ic wonders, either in collecting Hearbs, framing Sigils, Images, Lament, &c.
now by the bleſſing of Almighty God, withont whoſe providence we can performe no worthy act, I have produced to an end the ſecond part of my intended Work, and could have willingly acquieſced untill a further opportunity had been offered: but ſuch is the defire and importunity of ſeverall wel-affeted to this ſtudy, that beyond my firſt intentions 1 againe adventure upon the ſucceding Tratiate of NaT1 vr 116, wherein the pittifull and mercifull God of all the faith- full, whoſe brightneſſe ſhines in our fraile underſtandings, aſ- fiſt me, that I may performe this Work with judgment and un- derſtanding, for the good of all honeſt-hearted Engliſh, my moſt —— —ê for my Task is difnicult, and thy ſervant is of little waderſtanding ! few, nay none at all are the helps I expect from any man living PPP 3 (having

Vie of the Table.

The Reflification of « Nativity by Accidents, end framig of an Aſtrologicall Speculum,
Ome give rules for the rectifying of a Nativity by the Tran- Nins of the Planets upon the principall Hylegiacall places of che Nativity; others by a figure of Profetions directing there- in the aſcendant and Mid — to their Promittors : were there any cerrainty or aſſurance in either of theſe Wayes | would preſcribe them; but as I could never finde any verity or probability in either of thoſe two wayes in my praRiſe, ſo doe | leave them to any who are deſirous to practiſe them, and give directions to peruſe Origanus page 380. and John Schonar who magnifies the latter of theſe wayes by the ratification of his one Nativity, and Pezelius Page 226, &c. which Au- thors doe declare the practicall part thereot to thoſe delire it, &c.
He that would rectiſie a Nativity exactly, muſt verforme ic by tuch Accidents as have already happened to the N ative, be- fore you handle his Nativity; and to that purpoſe he muſt collect in readineſſe ſo many as poſſibly he can procure, and thoſe eminent ones, together with the certaine time, viz. the
rand month, and if poſſible the day when they happened,
t the moneth and yeer will well ſerve, if no neerer time can be obtained; for ſometimes we are inforced to accept of the yeer without the moneth. The quality of thoſe accidents oughe to be either ſuch misfortunes, lickneſſes, or caſualties as have happened to the body; and theſe are to be expected from the Aſcendants occurſe, progreſſion, or meeting with Male vo- lent Promittors 3 wherein you muſt confider the aſcend int being the place from whence we begin our worke, is called in this wor ke the Significator, as ſignifying ſuch or ſuch an ac- cident or ficknef iuall chance unto the Native, the Planet di- r=%:4 unto ci by body or alpect, is called the Promittor, and he ſhes the greatneſſe and quality of the accident or fickneſic, or promiſes to performe what the Sigailicacor de-

The Medium Celi is another Sigaifi - cator, and we direct him to his ſeverall Promittors for ho- uour, Preferment, Marriage, &c. and verily a Nativity can- not well be reQified but by Accidents belonging to the one or both thoſe Angles. The © in every Nativity is a principall fignificator, ſo is the ) and ©, yet a ſufficient rectification from theſe cannot be had: theſe five are called the Hyl:giacal! or p77 tp places of the Nativity; by direction whereof moſt of the affaires and contingencies belong n to every man or woman in a naturall courſe ot life are found out, both in uil'ty What, and in meaſure of tim: When.

Having framed your Speculum, you muſt place the characters of the twelve Signs in order as you ſee already done in the firſt or upper line of your work : the firſt column on the left hand where you find Deg. and Min. are the ſeverall degrees belong- ing to the Signes and houſes, where you find a ſecond number, it notes minutes adhering to the degrees.
Firſt, you muſt in every Signe, place tiſt Termes of every Planet in their proper degrees, as you find them in Pag. 104. as under V over againſt o. 54. you find t *, it tels you the Terms of * begin with no minutes of V, the 54. minuets do adhere to , as by and by ſhall be declared: then under Y
ver againſt the ſeventh degree of Y ; you find t , which tels you, that the Termes of 9 begin in the ſeventh degree of V: over againſt the fifreenth of J you find t , which fignifies the Termes of 7 begin there: over againſt the 22. of V, you find i, viz. & his Terms begins at the 22. of V: over againſt the 27 of V, you find th, viz, * his dominion in Terme be- gins at the 27, of V, and continues to the end of the Signe. You mutt underſtand, that in what degree onePlanet beginshis Terme, there the former leaves his power, and the other conti- nucs his vertue untill the next ſucceeds. And here | muſt ob- ſk rve and give you notice of a vulgar Ercrour committed by all or moſt of the Atiro/ogians, either late living, or at preſent now alive; that is, in direQing a Significator to the Termes of any Planet, they aii::ook commonly one degree, as for example, had they been to have di ected * ©, who in our Nativity is in 6. 37. =, tothe Termes of u, they did uſually in a = in che elevench degree thereof, whereas he hath no Terme in A untill he come to the tweltt degree thereof; for the firſt fix degrees of =, are fully belonging to h, from the end of x, or the ſeventh, eighth, ninth, tenth, elevench degree of =: are the Termes of , and then * enters, viz, at the twelſt degree.

Cnaye, CIII. Of the Sace of Life, or whetber the Native is like to live long, or not.
| 1s you proceed to any particular Direction of the five Hlegiaral places, you ought generally to conſider the ſtrength of the Poſiture of heaven, and therein whether the degree aſcending, the Lord of the Geniture, the © or , or the light of the time are extreamly afflicted, cc. for thole ar- gue no long life : it were therefore in vaine to frame long Di- rections upon that Nativity.
However, that which is principally confiderable,and ought if poſſible to be obtained, is, judiciouſly to examine the Nati- vicies of the Parents of the Childe, and whether the Significe- tors of Children in them ate ſtrong yea or notzfor as the good · neſſe of fruit depends upon the temperament of the root, ſo alſo Children, whoſe Parents have unfortunate Significators,, doe dye upon a ſmall ſickneſſe, &c. but fith they are not uſu- - attainable, you may proceed according to the Method following :
Firſt, confider the degree aſcending, which moſt properly hath fignification of Life, whether it be fortunate or not; ics then fortunate when in the termes or ſigne, or in the æ or A of a benevolent Planet; and fo on the contrary unfortunate , when either locally an infortune vltiates the degree aſcend- ing, or k his 0 or ꝙ aſpect; or when many violent fixed Starres of the nature of the Lord of the eight, doe ariſe with the degree aſcending, or are with or neer the Lumizary of the time. If according to theſe rul a you ſinde the Aſcend int for- tunate, the Childe may live paſt his infancy ; but if afflicted. he hardly eſcapes his very infancy.

Joba-nes Angelus, in the City of Venice, printed a Book, wherein he did deliver a little of the Manners of every Native according to the degree aſcending, with a fit Motto and Icon thereunto : but becauſe | conceive he was a little too ſtrict therein, I referre the Reader to judge of his Works; and have thought good to relate what I find in a general! way delivered.

But to put all this into practice, you muſt doe thne, if you would know from whom or whence the Native ſhall obrain- Wealth, or from whom lofle or damage ſhal proceed: In the firſt place, conſider the fortitude of the Significators, and how man of them are ſtrong, and whether more of them be well forcif- ed, or weak and unfortunate : for it all prove ſtrong and for- tunate, as it ſeldome doth, then judge according to the nature of every Planer, and houſe wherein they are, that the Native ſhall have furtherance either to procure an Eſtate or Fortune, or Meanes given him by _ ſignified by thoſe Houſes, whereby he may encreaſe his ſtore : It all the Significators be not ſtronꝑ, but the greater part, then judge according to the Signe and houſe they are in, together with the Nature of the Planets :

If there be as many Significators of Wealth imbecill as firong, they intimate a kind of unconſtant Fortune, and that the Na- tive ſhall at this time, by fuch Men and ſuch Commodities or meanes, encreaſe his Eſtate ; and at other times by ſuch or from ſuch, impoveriſh himſelſe, ſo that he ſhall neither abound with Wealth, or ever be in any diſtreſſe for want of ſubliſtence : for conſider in what condition of fortune his Arceſtors left him, and it's probable you find him in the ſame condition, neither very much augmenting his prive fortune, or by any negle& or iil hasbandry of his o ne diminiſhing his Patrimony.

The ſame manner of judgment ſhall you give if a malevo- lent Planet be Significatorof anEſtate,and placed in dignities of a Fortune.
If a malignant Planet by nature is Signifcator of Wealth, and conſtituted in the Dignities of the Fortunes, and yet nut- withſtanding (hall be Retrograde or Combuſt, becauſe that then the evill is conduplicated, the Native (hill attaine more of his Eſtate by unlawfull or indire& Proceedings, then by lawfull or warrantable.
On the contrary, if a good Planet be in the Dignicies of the Iffortunes, Retrograde or Combuſt, the man thrives more by unwarrantable meanes then otherwiſe.
So that you ſee this judgment depends upon four Conſide- tations.
Firſt, from the nature of the Significator, whether good or evill.
Secondly, from the nature of the Signe be or they occupy.
Thirdly, fram bis or their being or not being Cembuſt.
Fourthly, from being Retrograde or not Retrograde, for accordingly be — good or ill, warrantable or indirett meanes.

Concerning Parents, &c. And Patron of the Father,
AE. Significators of the Father in every Geniture are: Of the Father Fiſt, the fourth houſe; ſecondly, the Lord thereof:
thirdly, the © in a diurnall Genitare, h in a noctur-

If the Significators be unfortunate and impedited, they ſhery the dejected and low condition of the Father, and that he is ſubjet to many misfurtunes, &c.
If ſome be ſtrong and others weak, and they tend to a medi- ocrity, then the For: une of the Parents are intended or re- mitted according to the nature of the Planet or Planets oceu- pying the fourth houſe; and it there be many therein, he that is molt dignitied ſhall be preferred in judgment, But theſe judg- ments are belt drawn from the Fathers own Nativity.

Firſt, whether they be ſtrong or weak, or in aſpe& with the Fortunes, or not joyned with fixed Seirs of ill influence.
Secondly, whether they be free from the evill aſpects of h and .
Thirdly, whether the Temperament be equall or uneqnall.

CMA. CXL, Cauſe of TRAv ELL.
Irſt,the cauſes moving to travell are taken from the Planet: who are Significators of Journeys : Secondly, from the ſig- nification of the Houſes in which the Planets are polited : I ac- quainted yon before, the Planets ſigniſied Matters and Perſons, wherefore it thoſe Planets that delign travell are conjoyned to h, or are in his eſſentiall Dignities, or if h himſelſe be Sig- nificator,he ſhewes the cauſe of Travell to proceed from Matters or men Saturnine, viz. as for matters either for diſcovery of ſome rich Mines, or recovery of ſome Inheritance befallen him by the Deceaſed, or ſome ſuch Things or Commodities as are Saturnine. As tor Men, Ancient men, grave, reverend Magi- ſtrates, Gentlemen or Merchants of h his condition, &c.
If A*, he ſhewes the cauſe, it's for Religion, Honour, Law, Eccleſtaſticall preferment, &c. or for, or by perſons who ad- miniſter ſuch things, as Biſhops, Church. men, Prelates, No- nE Gentlemen, &c. and ſo ot the reſt of the Pla- nets, as ſpeciſied in the ſecoud Houſe.
Next conlider the fignification of the Houſcs in which the Planets are polited, who declare Journeys ; for if the Sig- nificators be in the firſt,a naturall deute of fecing ſtrange Cours tries poſſeſſeth the Native: In the ſecond, he travels with a deſire to buy and ſell, whereby he may cnrich himſclfe,%c. a d ſoin the reſt.
So that in few words, the cauſe of Travell appeares from the nature of the Significators, and naturall ſignification of the Houſes whereia they are placed.

Of the quality of Dreames.
If h be Significator, the Native's Dreames will be of matters and men Saturnine, viz. of Husbandry, rude matters, digging up che ground, finding of Treaſure of hollow Caves under the earth, dark places, Deſarts, Sepulchers of the Dead, Devils, Spirits, feartull things, cauling feare and horror, &c.
* Signifies pleaſant Dreames concerning the Gentry, or Preferment, Church-matters, perſons of quality, Kings, Prin ces, Noble. men, &c.
The Dreames he intimates, are of contentions, Fights, Emnities, Tumults, Horſes, Wars,burnings of Houſes, of Dogs, Hunting, &c.

Speciall Rules of che two Luminaries,
® and ) in the very degree of their Exaltation, free from the Infor- lunes, are arguments unto the Native of obtaining a Kingdome, if be be capable thereof.
The Luminary of the time being © in a diurnall Geniture, ) in a nodurnall, in Exaltation and in the mid-beaven,the Signe aſcending be- ing alſo Regal, and the Lord of the Signe that Light is in, placed in the aſcendant or mid-beaven, defignes Kingly Preferment, if the Native be of Kingly Progeny if be be not ſo borne, be ſhall have auibority in hind, according to bis Birth, like unte a King. |
I ben the Luminary of the time culminates with the degree of the mid- beaven, and is ſurrounded with the benevolent aſpecis of other Planets, the Native ſhall bave great command in the Kingdome : but if that Light have no Aſſi l ance from otber Planets, or that other Planets are not con- fituted neer the cuſp of the tenth, the Native will be in great favour with the King, er Chiefe without adminifiration of the affaires of the King- me, or next unto the Principall abont the King, with adminiftration of publike affaires. The Luminaries in angles not environed of the Planets, the Native ſball live in ſuch a condition, as bis Father or Anceſtors did. If the Luminaries be cadent, and the Planets aljo, the Native will be wretched, and onely converſant all the dayes of bis life amongſt valgar Perſons, br in Townes and Villages. |

Cnae. CXLVIII. Of the Magiftery, Exercitation or Profeſſion of the Native,
"A STRoOLoOGERS name the Magiſtery of the Native, a
Study or Delight, an Art or Action wherein any one leads his Life, gets his Living, preſerves his Eſtimation, and wherein he ſpends the principall part of his Life, whether it be publike, as of Kings or Princes, whereof ſome adminiſter Ju- ſtice ; others Military Exerciſes ; others Huntings ; others de- light in other actions; others in Philoſophy and Theology; others in the Mathematicks,
Or whether his Profeſſion be private, either learned from another, or attained by his owne induſtry, or mechanicall, la- borious, and for pleaſure ; far doubtleſſe every man hath incli- _ more or leſſe to ſome one Quality, Proſeſſion, &c. or other. |
Three things are wont to be conſidesed in this Judgement. Firſt, I betber the Native is to bave any Magiftery at all,viz. any Trade,
Studh of Frefeſſen ; or whether be ſhall be without any.  * |;

Of Venus when ſbe alone deſignes the Profeſſion.
She ſignifieth of her ſelfe Pleaſure, Delight, Tenderneſſe, De- licacy, Feaſtings, Gueſts, and all ſuch things as tickle the Sen- ſes with Pleaſure.
She is a great enemy to Learning and Diſcipline, nor is ſhe overcome — by h, men therefore wholly given to their Book are of harſh Manners,

Of MeRcunRyY and VENUS commixed,
If they fignifie the Art or Profeſſion, they diſcerne one very ingenious, performed by a voluble or pleaſint Tongue, the Native is loquacious, fickle, full of jollity ; they uſually make Muficians, ebpxcially when they are in a Veneriall Signe,Poets, Orators, Dancing Maſters, Tumblers or Dancers upon Ropes, Painters, makers of Complexion-water for ſoft Dames, Wax Chandlers, Limners, Writers of Stage-playes, &c. Of the quality of a violent Death,
Many things are herein conſiderable, that ſ@ we may give out Judgment with more certainty : for we muſt firſt ſee,and judge whether theNative ought to dye by a violentDeath yea or not.
Secondly, we muſt conſider the Manners of the Native,whe- ther he is like to be a Theefe or a Murderer, or will become an Hereticke; for where wickedneſſe in Nativities appeares, then by the Significators of a violent Death, publique puniſhments are ſignified,
Thirdly, the condition of men is obſervable ; for Princes ſeldome or never dye as the vulgar common man doth. *

To the O or £ of h.
This Direction diſcovers many Ilaborions, difficult and un- fortunate Miſchances unto the Native, deprives him of his for- mer Honour, Ofh-e, Magiſtracy, Employment or Reputation, by meanes of Saturnine and vulgar perſons, or of ſlye difiem: bling Courtiers, by Country Clowns, Husbandmen, Colliers, Day- labourers, &c. who ſeem all to conſpire and agree here- in againſt him: and as this portends unto vulgar perſons, Beg- gery, Poverty, Diſgrace, c. ſo unto People diſcredit, accuſa- tions, and trecherous Informations, damage by Thefts, &c. To a King orPrince,it portends breach of Leagues with his Allies, Tumults in his Dominions, a diſcontented Commonalty, a falſhearced Privy-Counſell, wholly deſtructive.

In ſtrange Countryes it menaceth the Native with various impediments in bis Life and Fortune, it ſtirres up Saturnine, aged men againſt him, and portends unto his Mother (if he have any alive) and to his Wife, danger of death.

Cnae,. CLXVI. Of the mea ure ef time in DinRECTIONS,
Efore l handle this point,” I muſt give this generall Inſtru-
tion to the younger ſonnes of Art, that in judging of the effects depending upon any Direction, they maturely conſider the age of the Native, for events are to be accommodated unto the difference of times, and therefore one ſhould extreamly de- ccive himſelfe,that upon any ſtrong Direction of the aſoendant or mid- heaven to the A or * or ꝗ of 9 or ), ſhould pre- di& Marriage unto a Native that is then but three, tour or five yeers of age; how much, I ſay, ſhould the Artiſt miſſe the mark if he ſhould attribute that action to an Infant, of which he can- not then be capable; or what a madneſſe were ic to predict to an aged man the begetting of a Child, when in reaſon it ſelſe, and by reaſon of his extreame feebleneſſe, no ſuch thing can ot may be expected we muſt therefore prognoſticate things poſ- fible and naturall, befitting and agreeing unto every one, ac» cording to the difference of his yeers, &c. We muſt alſo in all our predictions have the world to underſtand, that the com- mon or generall fate of any Nation or place, is of more efhca- cy then any ones particular: we muſt alſo conſider the Region where any one is borne,if we will exquiſitely judge of the ſhape and forme of the Native, and of themanners of his Mind, &c. ever conſidering the moſt powerfull cauſe, &c.
But now we come to handle the meaſure of timgin Dir« (ti- ons, wherein there are at this day three ſeverall Opinions, yet not ſuch as doe make any great difference in the marrer.
The opinion of Prelomey hath continued ſince his time untill this laſt Age without any coatradiftion ; and it was thus: If you would dire@ the aſcendant in any Nativity, you muſt — form it by the oblique aſcent ions belonging to the place where the Bi th was : the words of Prolomey, lib. 4. cap. alt. are theſe:
Com antem prerogatis ſwmitar ab Horoſcopo , demtur anni gradibus kngitadinis , equales aſcenfienibus. cujuſque Climatic : a an

He meanes no more, but that the al- cendant in every N :tivicy is to be directed by the oblique aſ- centions of the Climate ; the mid heaven by the right aicenti- ons the mealure of time is by allowing for every degree of the Aquator one yeer, for every minute thereof ſix dayes, &c.
