The ſecond meaſure of Time.
AwTonrus MacrNnus , an Ttalianof tingular Learns. ing, and one of the greateſt Mathematicians of Europe, was the 570 that queſtioned this meaſure of time, delivered unto Poſte - rity by Ptolomey, in pag. 5 1. of his Primum Mobile, printed 1604. induced hereunto (as he ſaith) by an Aphoriſme of Doctor Dee of Landon, and ſ elſe gathered from the Writings and opinion of the famous Tycho Brabe the Dane, &c, he concludes-
«bus: That the meaſure of time ought not to be taken or dedu- Vary 3. oed
710 The Effects of Directions. ced from the ſimple motion of the ©, but from his true or ap- parent motion, &c. and concludes, T hat in his opinion, we ought to take for the common meaſure of one yeers ſpace in the directions of every Sipnificator, that arke of the A quator agreeing to the apparent motion of the © at time of the birth according to his right aſcentions, and not oblique aſcentions ot the Region. The Pratlice,
{ Take the right aſcention of the © at the houre of the birth, as if it wereat noon ; adde againe the apparent diurnall moti- on of the © for the next day and ſame hour,and take his righe aſcention, then ſubduct the leſſer right aſcention from the greater, and what remaines is the difference of the revolution of the firſt mover from the diurnall revolution of the O, and ſhall be accepted for the meaſure of time for one yeer.
In our Nativity, pag. Foo. the place of the © is 6. 37. =, his right aſcention is 186. 4. the t motion of the © added to the ſame hour of the next day, makes it 7. 36. , its right aſcention 186. 58. the former right aſcention ſubſtracted from the latter, there reſteth as followes :
| 186 58 186 4 000 54
So then here is 5 4- minutes and no more, and thispropor- tion of the Æquator (hall be the meaſure of one yeer in the Di»  re&ions of our Nativity, according unto Maginus : We have added a generall Table, by meanes whereof, without taking the right aſcention belonging to the O, you may know that portion of the Xquator, w muſt be had in any Nativity,
for the yeerly meaſure of time: I doe the more y in- ſert this Meaſure, becauſe William Bredon, a late Miniſter of the - Church of and a ſingular Aftrologian, did wholly uſe
this Method. The Table followeth.

284. dayes : ſo that by this account, the Aſcendant did not come to the termes of ꝙ untill the Native
was 13. yeers compleat and three quarters currant in his fourteenth yeer, or thereabouts. The num- ber of degrees that the aſcendant is diſtant from the ꝙ of & are as aboveſaid 19. degr. 5 2. min.

And 48. min. give 324. dayes: ſo then the aſcen- dant by this Meaſure came not to the f of & until the 23. yeer curcant of this Natives age.
1965 The third and the laſt Meaſure of time which I | el now intend to handle, was*perfefted by Valentine 3% Naibods in his Coment of Prolomey, but commended and publiſhed by Mag inus himſelſe, as the more correct and cer- taine meaſure in his Book de Diebus Criticis, and in his new Ta- bles publiſhed 1619. This Method of Naibeds 1 doe uſe in thoſe Ngtivicies where | take or have ſufficient time allowed me for performance of one; otherwiſe, J uſe Ptolomies way, Which is performed without any trouble or intricacy : I preſent thee v ith the learned Na ods, which is very familiar and eafie, as by the Example it will appeate and in my'owne — it is the molt cx & ci meaſure chat hitherto hath been ſound out.

By Naihod's compute, I would know the meaſure of time agreeing to the 11. deg. and 55, min.

Cu Ar. CLXVI.
Of annuall Profecliont, and by what means to find out ibe Proſectianall Signe of every zeer,
»
PiA and Progreſſion are all one, and are no more then a regular or orderly change of the Significators according to the ſucceſſion of Signes.
Amnuall Progreſſion.
Profeftion is threefold ; one Annual, wherein we give thirty degrees, or one whole Signe to a ſolar yeer; as it in any ones Geniture there doth aſcend the ſixt degre: of , from the ſixt degree of I to the ſixt degree of & ſhall be the Profectionall
XXxXX 2 Signe
716 The Efeclr of Direcliass.
Signe the firſt yeer ; ia the ſec n yeer, the Profectionall Signe of the aſc-n4ant (hill be from the ſixt degree of S to the ſixt degree of S; in the third yeer, from the ſixt degree of & to the fixt degree of & hill be th: ProfeRti >nall figure of the aſ- cend int; and ſo uatill all the twelve Signes are ended, and then you mit begin again, for every twelft yeer the annual! Profections are the ſame : where you mutt note, that you muſt begin the ycer of Proſecti on, at what tim: the © returnes to the degree and minute he was in at the Radix, and for this cauſe it is called a Solar yeer : alſo, in every beginning you muſt have upon every cuſp of a kouſe the ſame degree and minute which was there in the Kealix, the Signe onely varied,
Monetbly Pro fecliont.
In menſurnall Profection, we give unto every moneth one Signe, ſo that the Signe of the annuall Proſection is the Signe profectionall of the firſt moneth ; the ſecond, of the ſecond moneth, and ſo in order; wherefore in that way, we divide the ſolar yeer into thirteen equall parts, whereof each is called a Profectionall mongths .
Diurnall Prof lion.
In diurnal] Profections, one Signe is given to two dayes three hours and fifty four minutes, &c. but this is more ſcrupw- lous then neceſſary. ä ©
' I will onely proceed to annuall Profections, holding this opinion, That if one ſhou'd follow the niceneſſe of the Ancient; in every particular, it were impoſſible to judge one Nativity ex- actly in halfe a ycers time.
Some doe teach how to make one generall Table of Profeti;- ent, which will ſerve for all Nativities, as Origanuw pag. 766, Maginus, pag. 52. Prim. Mob. &c. | ever held it beſt to frame a particular Table for every Nativity, which is as eaſily perform- ed, and more uſefull : This which followeth is the Profe&io- nall Table of our Nativity in pag. 500,

Of the Profections of the Aſcendant and Y, and what'they ſignifi in every Houſe,

come to the firſt Houſe, the N arive is uſually cheerful, and it implies a fic yeer to provide what is neceſſary for the B dy, either Cloathes, Victuals, xc.
When to the ſecond houſe, it's good to buy and ſell Com- modiries, and argues 4 _ time for encreaſe of Eſtate,
When to the third, the Native may proſper in Journeys, and it inclines him to converfe with his Kinred and religi- ous men.
When to the fourth, he mv expedt ſome Inheritance, a new Houſe,or a blefling from his Parents ; he may ſearch for Wealth out of the bowels of the Earth, ordeale in Mines, &c. he may build or repaire ; yet the Native may be ſorrowfull, fall of fears, in danger of Water, it's not good to goe long Jour-
„ Kc. hen to the fiſt, the Nite takes pleaſure according to his y es, either at Schuole with hivequals, ur with women, if ca- pable, or according to the yeers of his age when this happens; ** 220d to make Covenants, ſend Letters, perfect Accompes, put on new Cloathes.
When to the lx, he may feare a ſickneſſe, the hatred of ma- ny vulgar people, continuing a long time: beware of ill Sec- vants, make no Journeys, an ill time to deale in Merchandize, in Contrafts, in Bargaines, &c.
When to the ſeventh, the Native is froward and impatient, eaſily inclined to Women and wantonneſſe, many open ene- mies ariſe againſt him, it's good to marry, the other Significators concurring ; oppreſſed with wangling, vexed with iuirs or contentions : if the Native radically incline to Souldiery, it
now puts him forward, and inclines unto — ſuffer,
When to the eighth, either his Body or his Ge ſubj ect co be by Iyes, ſlanders, treacheries, and ſometimes L122 deagh, *
730 The Effefs of Directions. death, if the Hyleg cometo any mortall Direction: Ic is not good to make new Contracts, or to be too adventurous in T rade, &c.
When to the ninth, it points out a convenient time to un- dertake long Journeys, it's goed to ſtudy and to follow Learn- ing, and wha familiar with Church-men, to ſtudy Chymiltry, for it ſhewes a mind and fancy inclinable to cutioſities, &c.
When to the tenth, the Native is inclinable to be ambitious, or to endeavour Preferment, and to be converſant with emi- nent men, Magiſtratrs, Nobles, &c. and he (if capable) attains Preſetment, or publike employment in the Common-wealth; it notes a fit time in the Tradeſman to follow his employment wich induſtry, for it promiſeth him much encreaſe ; he may navigate or journey by Land or Sea with good ſafety and re- putation, ſhall be much eſteemed.
When to the eleventh, ic ſignifies a cheerfull heart and pcr- ſon, the encreaſe of the Native's Friends, it imports the yeer to be very proſperous in all worldly affalres, and it invites the Native to beſtirre himſe!fe and —— time well, whereby he may much advance his private fortune; &c.
When to the twelfr, the Native will be ſickly, yet hardly find out or diſcover the caule, be in continuall emnity with ill Neighbours, and none knowes for what ; he will be in danger of impriſonment or baniſhment,or hiding his head, unleſſe the twelſt houſe in the Radix was fortunate, it imports an unlucky yeer for dealing in great Cattle, and adviſeth the Native to be- ware of Horſmanſbip, Horſe. races, xc. The Ancients have here- in been tedious, and left very many rules, which by reaſon of being ill cranſlated out of the Arabic by ſuch as underſtocd nt Affrolegie, are in many things contradictory: I have there- ö fore omitted their further judgment upon the aſcendant, onely concluding wich this ſhort vale, That when the aſcendant comes to the body of an Iifortwne, viz. to the Signe wherein one was, conſider in what houſe of the Revolution that Infor- tune (als, becauſe the ill intended ſhall partake of the nature of thathauſe, &. they alſo, judge much by the Lord of the yeer, and the Lords of every houſe 5 yau. ſhall find in my judgment

When to the ſecond houſe, is intended the Native ſhall then receive the profics of his former yeers employment, and ſhall have in poſſeſlion that reward which was but formerly promiſed him.
When to the third houſe, the Native may expect but an in- different condicion of life: for in regard it's the ſixt houſe from the tenth, it intends more ill then good, viz. the loſſe of Kinred, haticd of malicious Ncighbours, or robbery in Jour- neyiu When to the fourth houſe, the Native's fame and eſtimation is called in queſtion, the Native and his Parents agree ill, and his Neighbours of more power then himſelſe doe ſwell,
When to the fift, he that hath children, will much rejoyte wich them ; he that hath none, inclines to be merry, jocund and wanton, to take his pleaſure, ſpending more then he gets.
When to the ſixt houſe, a convenient time and ſeaſon to agl- tate certaine affaires belonging to his Family and to Husban- dry 3 but nothing ſuccesfull to deile in matters or things ho- nourable,in which matter nothing will that yeer ſucced well. time then another, according to the directions of other Pla- nets coincident with any wherein the Lord of the aſcendant is concerned.
Our Native abſolutely is a melancholy perſon (per ſe) grave, auſtere, of a firme reſolution, ſolitary, laboriovs, taciturne, nothing loquacious, &c. The Q of & to h, induce him to be obſtinate and a little wilfull, a tincture of malice remaining in him ; for the Infortunes ace both fixed, &c. theſe are the natu- ral qualities belonging to h, as here polited and aſpected; be- ſides, ſome ſemale infirmeneſſe naturally are ingraſted in him, which h in g the houſe of , doth beſtow on him in the worſer ſenſc, for though there is reception betwixt 2 and h, yet is there no aſpect, and that wr ot ꝙ to bh is out of the ſeventh houſe, the naturall houſe Women, Loves, &c. and certainly theſe depraved manners would prevaile, and extreamly predominate even to the great detriment of the Na- tive, if that 7 and the) were not in an aytriall and humane Signe, and in A aſpect, whereby the Native is refrained by ui banity and education from diſcovering publickly, or making uſe of his native and occult Saturnine conditions;and he in his — yeers, untill h have finiſhed his firſt revolution, will
e more tractable, more given to his delight, to be more neat and cleanc in his apparell, to trimand adorne himſelfe, to be onely carefull of a competent fortune, to be graceſull, lovely and majeſticall in his d-portment : theſe later mentioned qua- lities are of the nature of 9 , ſhe being Lady of the Geniture.
The A of & and ) incite him unto Study and Learning, unto Travell and ſee many Countries: ſo that in concluſion, thus much is derived from the Politure of Heaven, that his Manners are ſufficiently laudable, managed totally after an auſtere and grave Comportment; at ſome times betwixt merry
and x7 rs ark at other times wholly oppreſſed with fad- neſſe, and againe accidentally and for a ſhort ſeaſon pleaſantly merry; for upon the Direction of » to * his aſpeX or Terms, he ſhall find himſelſe more or leſſe inclinable to covetouſneſſe, folitarineſfe,pertinacy, and what in the vulgar Engliſh we call doggednefle; but when the ) either by Direction comes to the body or aſpect, vr termes of 25 oreither of her houſes is the | | | alcendant

I ſee no evill influence threatned by this Revolution, but the contrary. The Profectionall Revolution being the ſame at the Birth, inſinuate the Native to be very cheerfull, luſtily intent to proſecute his affaires in the Common - wealth, &c.
